articles.label: Investigation
articles.headline: Pensacola's $2.3M sidewalk gap: Why the city keeps funding studies instead of concrete
articles.dek: Since 2019, the city has commissioned four separate pedestrian connectivity studies. Zero miles of new sidewalk have been built in District 6. Council votes on another consultant contract Thursday.
brief: Pensacola has spent $2.3 million on pedestrian connectivity studies since 2019 while building no new sidewalks in District 6
City Council will vote Thursday on a $485,000 contract with Kimley-Horn for a fifth study covering the same neighborhoods
District 6 residents have submitted 847 sidewalk requests since 2020, more than any other district
articles.body:
The corner of Langley Avenue and Navy Boulevard has no sidewalk. It hasn't had one for thirty years. Children walk in the street to reach Warrington Elementary. Seniors from the nearby apartment complex push walkers along the shoulder. A faded memorial cross marks where 67-year-old Delores Washington was struck and killed by a pickup truck in February 2023.

The city has studied this intersection four times.

Since 2019, Pensacola has paid consultants $2.3 million to analyze pedestrian connectivity in neighborhoods west of I-110. The studies produced 1,247 pages of recommendations, three priority matrices, and a color-coded GIS map showing "critical gap areas" in red. The Langley and Navy intersection appears in all four documents. It remains marked as a priority.

No concrete has been poured.

On Thursday, City Council will vote on a $485,000 contract with Kimley-Horn & Associates for what city documents describe as a "comprehensive pedestrian network assessment" of Districts 5 and 6. The study area overlaps significantly with the 2019 Gresham Smith study, the 2021 Volkert assessment, and the 2023 Toole Design Group analysis.

"At some point you have to stop studying and start building," said District 6 Council member Teniade Broughton at last week's agenda conference. "My constituents can't walk on a consultant report."

The numbers

Public Works records obtained through a public records request show the city received 847 sidewalk installation requests from District 6 residents between January 2020 and March 2026. That's more than any other district. District 1, which includes downtown and East Hill, received 312 requests during the same period.

The city built 4.2 miles of new sidewalk citywide in that timeframe. None of it was in District 6.

The new construction concentrated in three areas: the Spring Street corridor near Cordova Mall, sections of 12th Avenue in East Hill, and a half-mile stretch of Scenic Highway near Bayview Park. All three projects were tied to private development agreements that required the builders to fund sidewalk installation.

City-funded sidewalk construction—using general fund or Local Option Sales Tax revenue—has effectively stopped since 2021. Public Works Director Kesha Rhodes-Mills confirmed in a March 18 memo to council that the department has no active sidewalk construction projects and no funded projects in the capital improvement plan for fiscal years 2027 or 2028.

"We are in an assessment phase," Rhodes-Mills wrote. "The upcoming study will allow us to prioritize investments strategically."

Study after study

The pattern began in 2019 when the city hired Nashville-based Gresham Smith to conduct a $340,000 "walkability audit" of west Pensacola. The 186-page report, delivered in August 2020, identified 23 miles of "high-priority" sidewalk gaps and recommended a phased construction approach starting with school routes.

The city did not implement the recommendations. Instead, it hired Volkert Inc. in March 2021 for a $425,000 "pedestrian safety study" covering much of the same geography. That report, completed in November 2021, reached similar conclusions.

Then came the 2022 federal infrastructure bill. Pensacola applied for a $3.8 million Safe Streets for All grant to fund sidewalk construction in Districts 5 and 6. The application cited the Gresham Smith and Volkert studies as evidence of planning work.

The city didn't get the grant. The rejection letter, dated March 2023, noted that the application "lacked sufficient implementation specificity" and recommended "additional planning to develop shovel-ready projects."

So the city hired another consultant. Toole Design Group received a $510,000 contract in June 2023 to develop what the scope of work called "implementation-ready designs" for priority sidewalk segments. The firm delivered a 340-page report in February 2024.

No construction followed.

Last month, city staff presented the Kimley-Horn contract as necessary preparation for a second Safe Streets for All application due in September. The scope of work describes a "network gap analysis" and "prioritization framework"—language nearly identical to the three previous studies.

Where the money goes

The $2.3 million spent on pedestrian studies since 2019 would have funded approximately 2.8 miles of standard sidewalk construction at current Public Works cost estimates of $820,000 per mile.

Pensacola's total sidewalk network spans about 289 miles. The city estimates it has 147 miles of gaps—areas where sidewalks are required by code but don't exist. At current construction costs, filling every gap would require roughly $120 million.

The city's entire capital improvement budget for fiscal year 2026 is $48 million. Sidewalks are not among the listed projects.

Mayor D.C. Reeves, who took office in November 2022, has emphasized "data-driven decision making" in infrastructure spending. At a January town hall in Brownsville, he defended the study approach.

"We can't just pour concrete randomly," Reeves said in response to a question about sidewalk delays. "We need to understand the full picture before we commit taxpayer resources."

Broughton, who represents the district with the most sidewalk requests and zero new construction, offered a different view at the March 25 council meeting.

"The full picture is that people are walking in the street," she said. "The full picture is that woman who died on Navy Boulevard. We have enough pictures."

Thursday's vote

The Kimley-Horn contract appears as item 7B on Thursday's regular council agenda. City staff recommends approval.

The contract would run 18 months, with a final report due in October 2027—eight years after the first study began and one month after the next Safe Streets for All grant deadline.

Council members Delarian Wiggins and Allison Patton have both indicated they may vote against the contract. Wiggins, who represents District 5, said at last week's agenda conference that he wanted to see "actual construction dollars" in any sidewalk-related expenditure.

Broughton has not said publicly how she will vote. She did not respond to a request for comment.

The intersection at Langley and Navy remains without a sidewalk. The memorial cross for Delores Washington has faded in the sun. Someone recently replaced the plastic flowers.

The city's records show four studies have recommended improvements there. A fifth study, if approved Thursday, would likely recommend the same.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: The Cordova Park teardown boom: Inside the quiet rezoning requests reshaping a 1960s neighborhood
articles.dek: Twelve demolition permits in 18 months. Modest ranch homes replaced by two-story builds maxing out lot lines. Neighbors say they weren't notified. County records show they were.
brief: Escambia County has issued 12 demolition permits in Cordova Park since October 2024, with replacement homes averaging 2,400 square feet compared to the original 1,200-square-foot ranches
County records confirm certified mail notifications were sent to adjacent property owners for each variance request, though several residents claim they never received them
The teardown trend is driven by investors purchasing properties at $180-220 per square foot and selling new builds at $350-400 per square foot
articles.body:
The yellow excavator arrived on Doris Avenue at 7:15 on a Tuesday morning in February. By noon, the 1,100-square-foot ranch house that Mildred Hensley had lived in for 43 years was a pile of concrete and splintered wood. By 4 p.m., the debris trucks were gone.

Three doors down, Barbara Whitman watched from her carport. She had no idea the demolition was coming.

"Nobody told us anything," Whitman said. "One day there's a house. The next day there's a hole in the ground."

Escambia County records tell a different story. Certified mail notification for a setback variance on the Hensley property was sent to Whitman's address on January 8. The return receipt shows a signature dated January 12. Whitman says it isn't hers.

This disconnect—between what neighbors say they know and what county records say they were told—sits at the center of a quiet transformation reshaping one of Pensacola's most established neighborhoods.

The numbers

Since October 2024, Escambia County has issued 12 demolition permits within the Cordova Park subdivision boundaries. Eight replacement homes have been completed. Four are under construction. The pattern is consistent: 1,100 to 1,400 square foot single-story ranches built between 1958 and 1965, replaced by two-story homes ranging from 2,200 to 2,800 square feet.

Ten of the 12 projects required variance requests. Nine sought reduced side setbacks. Six requested increased lot coverage beyond the 35% maximum. All ten were approved by the Escambia County Board of Adjustment.

The Flightline reviewed variance application files for all ten properties. Each contained proof of certified mail notification to adjacent property owners. Each contained affidavits of compliance with Florida Statute 125.66 public notice requirements. Eight of the ten hearings drew no public comment. Two drew objections from neighbors who attended.

Both objections were overruled.

The economics

The math driving the teardown boom is straightforward. Cordova Park sits between Bayou Texar and Cordova Mall, five minutes from Sacred Heart Hospital and eight minutes from downtown. The neighborhood's mature live oaks, established street grid and proximity to A-rated Cordova Park Elementary make it attractive to young families priced out of East Hill and North Hill.

Problem is, the houses don't fit the market.

"You've got 1960s construction on these lots," said Marcus Webb, a residential appraiser with Gulf Coast Valuation Services. "Single-pane windows, original electrical, no insulation to speak of. The cost to renovate to modern standards often exceeds the cost to demolish and rebuild."

Webb provided data on recent Cordova Park transactions. In 2025, teardown properties—homes purchased and demolished within 90 days—sold for an average of $198 per square foot of lot area. New construction on those same lots sold for an average of $372 per square foot of finished living space.

A typical transaction: A 1,200-square-foot ranch on a 9,000-square-foot lot sells for $265,000. Demolition and new construction costs run $320,000. The finished 2,400-square-foot home sells for $890,000. Gross profit before carrying costs: approximately $305,000.

"The incentive structure is clear," Webb said. "These aren't homeowners doing this. These are investors."

The builders

Three builders account for nine of the 12 demolition projects. Gulf South Development LLC, registered to a Navarre address, has completed four. Emerald Coast Custom Homes has completed three. A third company, Saltwater Properties LLC, has two under construction.

Florida Division of Corporations records show all three companies were registered within the past three years. Gulf South Development's registered agent is a paralegal service in Tallahassee. Emerald Coast Custom Homes lists a P.O. box in Pensacola. Saltwater Properties lists a residential address in Tiger Point.

The Flightline made contact attempts with all three builders. Gulf South Development did not respond to phone messages or emails. A representative for Emerald Coast Custom Homes declined to comment. Saltwater Properties' listed phone number was disconnected.

The notification question

Florida law requires local governments to notify adjacent property owners of variance requests by certified mail at least 15 days before a hearing. Escambia County's development services department maintains records of these notifications.

The Flightline reviewed notification records for all ten Cordova Park variance requests. Each file contained copies of certified mail receipts. Seven showed signatures. Three showed "unclaimed" notations after multiple delivery attempts.

Yet interviews with 14 adjacent property owners revealed a consistent theme: They didn't know.

"I'm home all day," said Gerald Patterson, whose property borders a lot on Baisden Road where a new two-story home now stands 4.2 feet from his fence line. "Nobody came to my door. I never signed anything."

Patterson's certified mail receipt, obtained through a public records request, shows a signature dated September 14, 2025. Patterson examined the signature at The Flightline's office. "That's not my handwriting," he said. "Not even close."

Escambia County spokesperson Rachel Hendricks said the county follows notification requirements as prescribed by state law. "We send the certified mail to the addresses on file with the property appraiser," Hendricks said. "What happens after it reaches the postal service is outside our control."

Asked whether the county investigates disputed signatures, Hendricks said that would be a matter for law enforcement.

The Flightline contacted the Escambia County Sheriff's Office to ask whether any complaints had been filed regarding fraudulent certified mail signatures in connection with variance notifications. A spokesperson said the office had no record of such complaints.

At the hearings

The Escambia County Board of Adjustment meets the second Thursday of each month in the Ernie Lee Magaha Government Building. Meetings typically last 90 minutes. Variance requests are heard in order of application date.

The Flightline attended the March 12, 2026 meeting, where two Cordova Park variance requests were on the agenda. The first, for a property on Barcelona Street, sought a side setback reduction from 7.5 feet to 4 feet. The second, for a property on Doris Avenue, sought increased lot coverage from 35% to 41%.

Both hearings followed identical patterns. A representative for the builder presented the request. Staff recommended approval, citing consistency with recent variances in the area. Board members asked no questions. No members of the public spoke. Both requests were approved unanimously in under three minutes each.

Board chair Thomas Langford, reached by phone after the meeting, said the approval pattern reflects the nature of the requests. "These are minor variances," Langford said. "They're consistent with what's already been built in the area. If nobody objects, there's no reason to hold things up."

Asked whether the board had concerns about the cumulative effect of variances on neighborhood character, Langford said that wasn't the board's role. "We look at each application individually," he said. "Cumulative impact is a planning question, not a variance question."

The neighbors who showed up

Not every hearing passed without comment.

On November 14, 2025, Carol and Dennis Markham appeared before the Board of Adjustment to oppose a setback variance for a property adjoining theirs on Inverness Drive. The Markhams had lived in their home since 1987. The proposed new construction would place a two-story wall 4.5 feet from their bedroom window.

"We presented photos, diagrams, everything," Carol Markham said in an interview. "We explained how the new house would block our light, how we'd be looking at a wall instead of the sky."

The board approved the variance 5-0.

"They thanked us for our input and then voted like we hadn't said anything," Dennis Markham said. "What's the point of showing up?"

The Flightline reviewed minutes from all Board of Adjustment meetings since January 2024. Of 47 variance requests for residential properties countywide, 45 were approved. The two denials involved commercial properties.

What comes next

Driving through Cordova Park in early April, the pattern is visible on nearly every block. Original ranches with jalousie windows and carports sit next to new two-story construction with impact-rated windows and two-car garages. The scale differences are jarring. A 1,100-square-foot house with an 8-foot roof peak neighbors a 2,600-square-foot house with a 24-foot roof peak.

Escambia County's Comprehensive Plan, last updated in 2019, designates Cordova Park as "Low Density Residential" with a maximum of six units per acre. The existing lot sizes—typically 8,000 to 10,000 square feet—accommodate this density. The plan says nothing about building height, setbacks or lot coverage beyond what's specified in the land development code.

County Commissioner Lumon May, whose district includes Cordova Park, said he's heard from constituents about the changes. "There's frustration," May said. "People feel like the character of their neighborhood is changing and they have no say in it."

Asked whether the county might consider overlay zoning or other measures to address neighborhood compatibility, May said that would require a comprehensive plan amendment. "That's a heavy lift," he said. "You're talking about a year-long process minimum. And property rights advocates would fight it hard."

For now, the teardowns continue. Building permit records show three more demolition applications pending for Cordova Park properties. Two are from Gulf South Development. One is from a company called Coastal Elevation Partners LLC, registered in January 2026 to an address in Panama City.

Barbara Whitman, the neighbor who watched the Hensley house come down in February, said she's considering selling. "Forty-one years I've been here," she said. "But this isn't the neighborhood I moved into. Pretty soon it won't be a neighborhood at all. Just a bunch of tall houses with strangers in them."

She paused. "I suppose that's progress."

articles.label: MILITARY
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week arrives with new flight path complaints from East Hill
articles.dek: Practice runs resume Monday ahead of the July show. A Facebook group tracking noise complaints has grown to 1,400 members. Base public affairs says the routes haven't changed since 2018.
brief: Blue Angels practice flights resume Monday at NAS Pensacola ahead of the July 11-12 homecoming airshow
A Facebook group called 'East Hill Flight Path Watch' has grown to 1,400 members documenting noise complaints since February
NAS Pensacola public affairs maintains flight routes have remained unchanged since 2018, citing FAA coordination records
articles.body:
The rumble started at 11:47 a.m. on a Tuesday in March. Patricia Wellborn was on a client call in her home office on Barcelona Street when the first F/A-18 Super Hornet screamed overhead. The windows rattled. Her dog bolted under the bed. The call dropped.

"I've lived in East Hill for 22 years," Wellborn said. "I know what the Blue Angels sound like. This was different. This was directly over my house."

Wellborn is one of 1,400 members who have joined "East Hill Flight Path Watch," a Facebook group created in February to document what residents describe as a noticeable shift in Blue Angels practice routes over their historic neighborhood. The group has logged more than 200 individual complaints, complete with timestamps, decibel readings from smartphone apps, and shaky videos of jets banking over Spanish-tile rooftops.

The Blue Angels return to full practice Monday ahead of their July 11-12 homecoming airshow at NAS Pensacola. It will be the first sustained practice schedule since the complaints began gaining traction online.

What the Navy says

Lt. Commander Rachel Stockton, public affairs officer for NAS Pensacola, said the demonstration team's flight patterns have not changed.

"The Blue Angels coordinate all practice airspace and routes with the FAA," Stockton said in an emailed statement. "Our current show box and practice corridors are the same ones we've used since the 2018 season, when we transitioned to the Super Hornet."

Stockton said the base has received 14 formal noise complaints through its community relations office since January. She declined to characterize the Facebook group activity, saying the Navy responds to complaints submitted through official channels.

The Flightline requested FAA airspace coordination records for Blue Angels practice routes from 2018 to present. That request is pending.

What residents hear

East Hill sits roughly two miles northwest of NAS Pensacola's runways. The neighborhood has coexisted with naval aviation noise since the base opened in 1914. Residents say they understand the tradeoff. Many moved there because of the base, not despite it.

"I'm not anti-military. My father was a naval aviator," said Tom Kirchner, a retiree who lives on La Rua Street. "But something changed this year. The jets are lower. They're louder. They're coming right over the neighborhood instead of staying over the water."

Kirchner said he began keeping a log in February after a practice run set off three car alarms on his block. He uses a sound meter app on his phone. His readings have topped 98 decibels during low passes—comparable to a chainsaw at close range.

The Facebook group's administrator, a software engineer named Derek Loomis, created a shared spreadsheet where members can input complaint data. Loomis said he started the group after seeing isolated complaints on Nextdoor and wondering if others noticed the same thing.

"I figured it was just me," Loomis said. "Then I posted asking if anyone else heard louder jets. I had 400 responses in two days."

The Super Hornet question

The Blue Angels transitioned from the F/A-18 Legacy Hornet to the F/A-18 Super Hornet in 2021, completing the first full season with the new aircraft in 2022. The Super Hornet is larger and produces more thrust than its predecessor.

Boeing, the aircraft's manufacturer, lists the Super Hornet's maximum thrust at 44,000 pounds with afterburner, compared to 32,000 pounds for the Legacy Hornet. Navy officials have said the demonstration team modified the new aircraft for airshow performance, but specific noise output comparisons are not publicly available.

Stockton said the Super Hornet transition did not prompt route changes. "The aircraft performs the same maneuvers within the same airspace," she said.

Residents are skeptical.

"If the routes are the same and the planes are louder, then the impact is different," Wellborn said. "That's just math."

No formal review planned

Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes East Hill, said he has received calls about jet noise but has not seen data suggesting a policy change is needed.

"The Blue Angels are part of Pensacola's identity," Bergosh said. "I'm sympathetic to noise concerns, but I'm not aware of any formal request to review flight paths. That would be a federal matter anyway."

Pensacola City Councilwoman Allison Patton, whose District 6 covers East Hill, said she plans to attend a community meeting the Facebook group is organizing for late April. She said she has not taken a position on the complaints.

"I want to hear from residents and get the facts," Patton said. "If there's a legitimate concern, we'll figure out who to talk to."

The Facebook group's meeting is scheduled for April 26 at the Bayview Community Center. Loomis said he has invited representatives from NAS Pensacola and the city council. As of Friday, no one had confirmed attendance.

Practice flights resume Monday at 11:30 a.m. The full schedule runs through June, with performances most Tuesdays and Wednesdays, weather permitting. The homecoming airshow is free and open to the public.

Wellborn said she'll be watching from her backyard with her sound meter app open.

"I love the Blue Angels," she said. "I just want someone to explain why they're flying over my house now."
